content_signals.body_excerpt:
Problem
Issue https://github.com/base/base/issues/3274 describes a divergence between builder execution and consumer re-execution of flashblocks. Per ron137's analysis:

~23% of flashblocks diverge despite identical tx set, ordering, parent hash, basefee, timestamp, and spec ID
Gas-only divergence (~8-14k extra gas on refund-heavy txs like ERC-4337 EntryPoint handleOps) — logs are byte-identical, only the checksum breaks
Content-drift on swaps — same pool address and topics but different sqrtPrice/amounts in logs
The dev team has confirmed (in https://github.com/base/base/issues/2265) that receipts will not return to the flashblock payload, so the fix must come from aligning the re-execution path with the builder's execution.

Root Cause
In crates/builder/core/src/flashblocks/payload.rs, the builder's build_block function already computes a FlashblockAccessList via FBALBuilderDb during execution. This access list tracks every account touched (balance/nonce/code/storage reads and writes) across the flashblock's transactions:

rust
let fal_builder = std::mem::take(&mut info.extra.access_list_builder);
let _access_list = fal_builder.build(min_tx_index, max_tx_index); /...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
Stacked on #3527 (`fix/follow-safe-coherent-read`); rebase to `main` once that merges.

#3527 stops follow mode from *promoting* a non-canonical block to `safe`, but a node that has *already* diverged still wedges. This PR adds recovery: on a safe-head divergence, find the highest block local and source agree on (the common ancestor), reset the engine's forkchoice down to it (Valid — the EL has that block, no P2P sync), then replay source payloads forward so the EL rebuilds onto the canonical chain. Mirrors op-node's `FindL2Heads` + reset model.

- Finality is never rewound: a finalized-head divergence is fatal (`FinalizedDivergence`); a reset below finalized is refused (`ReorgBelowFinalized`).
- Trust model inherited from #3527 (source authoritative for L2, finalized-guarded). A transient safe-head flap across source replicas is tolerated: recovery fires only on a genuine hash divergence at a given height, not when a replica briefly reports a stale-but-canonical head (that just yields Skip/Promote).
- Adds a stateful test EL so recovery tests fail if the design regresses to forkchoice-to-tip.

impact=sev3

This pull request implements transient RPC fetcher retries using the workspace's `base-retry` utility and addresses repository code quality/styling guidelines (CLAUDE.md) across multiple crates.

## Detailed Changes

### 1. RPC Fetcher Retry Logic
- Added `base-retry` and `backon` to dependencies in `base-proof-succinct-host-utils` (`crates/proof/succinct/utils/host/Cargo.toml`).
- Wrapped the HTTP JSON-RPC POST request in `fetch_rpc_data` (`fetcher.rs`) inside an exponential backoff retry configuration (3 attempts, starting at 500ms backoff and capping at 5 seconds).
- Integrated structured warnings via `tracing::warn!` to notify when RPC fetcher attempts fail before initiating a retry.

- `elfs`
- `ethereum-host`
- `ethereum-client`
- `range-program-utils`
- `client-utils`
- `scripts-utils`
- `prove-script`

### 3. Nested Import Cleanups
Relocated nested `use` statements from inside function bodies and closures ...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Follow mode could promote a **non-canonical block to `safe`**. This PR ships the **prevention** fix (a coherent read). Recovery for an already-diverged node is intentionally split out — see "Out of scope / follow-up".

`update_safe_and_finalized` read each source label in **two RPC calls** — `get_block_number(Safe)` for the height, then a separate by-number `get_block_info(number)` for the hash to compare. Against a load-balanced, multi-replica source (`BASE_NODE_SOURCE_L2_RPC`) these reads can hit **different backends in different reorg states**, pairing a canonical safe *height* from one replica with a stale/bad by-*number* hash from another, and promoting a non-canonical block to `safe` (the Zeronet incident).

Fix: read each safe/finalized label with a **single coherent `get_block_info(tag)`** (number + hash from one response) and verify the local block at that height against that hash. Mirrors op-node's `L2BlockRefByLabel`. A within-poll split is now structurally impossible.

Background write-up: `docs/blog/2026-06-12-zeronet-beryl-downgrade-fork.md`.

## Behavior

- In range and hashes match → promote.
- Source label **ahead of local latest** → **skip** (can't ...

let _access_list = fal_builder.build(min_tx_index, max_tx_index); /...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
Stacked on #3527 (`fix/follow-safe-coherent-read`); rebase to `main` once that merges.

#3527 stops follow mode from *promoting* a non-canonical block to `safe`, but a node that has *already* diverged still wedges. This PR adds recovery: on a safe-head divergence, find the highest block local and source agree on (the common ancestor), reset the engine's forkchoice down to it (Valid — the EL has that block, no P2P sync), then replay source payloads forward so the EL rebuilds onto the canonical chain. Mirrors op-node's `FindL2Heads` + reset model.

- Finality is never rewound: a finalized-head divergence is fatal (`FinalizedDivergence`); a reset below finalized is refused (`ReorgBelowFinalized`).
- Trust model inherited from #3527 (source authoritative for L2, finalized-guarded). A transient safe-head flap across source replicas is tolerated: recovery fires only on a genuine hash divergence at a given height, not when a replica briefly reports a stale-but-canonical head (that just yields Skip/Promote).
- Adds a stateful test EL so recovery tests fail if the design regresses to forkchoice-to-tip.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Eliminates multi-second sequencer stalls during batch derivation. Under load (e.g. consolidating a 4000+ block span batch), the sequencer's block production could be delayed by 3+ seconds, blowing past the block deadline. This fix makes reliable block production at 1s block times feasible even under heavy derivation load.

Closes #19263

## Journey & final root cause

The fix evolved through several iterations as we discovered deeper layers of the problem:

1. **First layer: event queue starvation.** The sequencer-engine path emitted 6+ events per block through the event system. Derivation events would pile up ahead of them, causing 200ms+ FCU-with-attrs delays.
   - **Fix:** Bypass the event system for the sequencer-engine path. Added direct-call methods (`StartBuild`, `SealBuild`, `ProcessPayload`) on `EngineController`, invoked synchronously by the sequencer.

2. **Second layer: seal phase still delayed.** Even with direct calls for build-start, the seal phase timer fired mid-drain during large span batches. The main `select` loop couldn't service `sequencerCh` until `Drain()` returned.
   - **Fix:** Replace `Drain()` with `DrainUntil()` + `RunAction()` direct cal...

## Summary

`L2Client.L2BlockRefBy{Label,Number,Hash}` and `SystemConfigByL2Hash` previously fetched the entire L2 block (`eth_getBlockByX` with `fullTx=true`) just to read a handful of header fields and decode the first transaction (the L1 info deposit). This PR introduces a minimal-fetch path that downloads only the header (`fullTx=false`) plus the first transaction in a single batched JSON-RPC round trip. By-hash is naturally consistent. content_signals.body_excerpt:
## TL;DR

OP Stack headers use **op-geth receipts-trie rules** (pre-Canyon: **omit `deposit_nonce`** in the trie leaf even when it’s on the in-memory deposit receipt). Reth’s **background receipt-root task** builds the trie with **`encode_2718`**, which **includes** the nonce → wrong root. Consensus **trusted that precomputed `(receipts_root, bloom)` when `Some`**, so nodes **rejected valid blocks** (`receipt root mismatch`). This PR fixes trie hashing for OP and **stops using `receipt_root_bloom` for validation** (parameter kept for upstream API only).

---

## Problem

### Trie encoding mismatch (Regolith, pre-Canyon)

For deposit receipts after Regolith, **`deposit_nonce`** may be present on the execution receipt, but **before Canyon** the receipts trie must match **op-geth `Receipts.EncodeIndex`**: trie value matches the usual typed receipt shape and **does not include** `deposit_nonce` when **`deposit_receipt_version` is unset**. After Canyon, **`deposit_receipt_version`** is set and the nonce is included in the trie per spec.

Generic tooling (e.g. **`encode_2718` → ordered trie**) encodes the nonce → **different `receiptsRoot`** than the block header for other...

## Summary

- Make the DA challenge contract optional for Keccak256 commitments, supporting permissioned Validium chains where ZK proofs (e.g., op-succinct/SP1) provide cryptographic data integrity without on-chain challenges
- Previously, `validateAltDAConfig` required `DAChallengeAddress != address(0)` for Keccak256, forcing users to pass a dummy address as a workaround

In a permissioned Validium model, the chain operator runs the DA server and ZK proofs verify `keccak256(data) == commitment` inside the zkVM. The on-chain challenge mechanism is redundant because there is no untrusted party to challenge and data integrity is already proven cryptographically.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
This PR addresses a state inconsistency in `op-revm` where deposit transactions of type `Create` in the Bedrock hardfork (Pre-Regolith) failed to increment the sender's nonce if the execution resulted in a `Halt` (e.g., `OutOfGas`).

### The Bug
In `OpHandler::execution_result`, halted deposits were only converted to bubble-up errors if the `REGOLITH` hardfork was enabled. For `BEDROCK`, the handler returned `Ok(Halt)`, which bypassed the `catch_error` logic where the nonce bump occurs.

### The Fix
1. Modified `OpHandler::execution_result` to return an `Err(OpTransactionError::HaltedDeposit)` for all halted deposit transactions.
2. Renamed `OpTransactionError::HaltedDepositPostRegolith` to `OpTransactionError::HaltedDeposit` for correctness across forks.

Verified with regression test `test_halted_deposit_tx_bedrock_nonce_bump`.
@rakita

content_signals.body_excerpt:
**Description**

Tolerate transient `get_logs` failures at the L1 head in the kona node-service `L1WatcherActor`.

The `head_stream.next()` → `l1_provider.get_logs(filter.select(head.hash))` sequence in `actors/l1_watcher/actor.rs` is racy on chains with rapid head reorgs or load-balanced RPC endpoints with propagation lag: the head hash can disappear from the responding node between the two calls, and the upstream returns `-32001 block not found`. The `?` propagates it, the actor task returns `Err` and is dropped, which closes `block_signer_sender` and pulls down the network actor too — surfaced by `rollup_node` as `Critical error in sub-routine`.

Demote this single `get_logs` failure to `warn!` + `continue`. The SystemConfig log scan is idempotent across head ticks, so skipping one is safe and the next head update retries. All other error paths in the watcher are unchanged.

**Additional context**

Most visible against fast L1s and load-balanced RPC endpoints where the node serving `get_logs` lags the node that emitted the head. The error is transient by construction, so retrying on the next tick is the correct recovery.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Replace bare `return err` calls in `recordsBook.prune()` with wrapped
errors using `fmt.Errorf("%w")` to include context about which operation failed.

## Motivation

Without context, errors from `prune()` are indistinguishable in logs —
a single `return err` gives no indication whether the failure was during
query, batch creation, record deserialization, or commit. This makes it
difficult to diagnose peer store issues in production nodes.

## Changes

- Query: `"failed to query records for pruning"`
- Batch init: `"failed to create batch for pruning"`
- UnmarshalBinary: `"failed to unmarshal record during pruning"`
- Batch commit (loop): `"failed to commit prune batch"`
- Batch re-init (loop): `"failed to create new batch during pruning"`
- Batch delete: `"failed to delete expired record <key>"`
- Final commit: `"failed to commit final prune batch"`

## Testing

`go build ./op-node/p2p/store/...` passes cleanly.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Eliminates multi-second sequencer stalls during batch derivation. Under load (e.g. consolidating a 4000+ block span batch), the sequencer's block production could be delayed by 3+ seconds, blowing past the block deadline. This fix makes reliable block production at 1s block times feasible even under heavy derivation load.

Closes #19263

## Journey & final root cause

The fix evolved through several iterations as we discovered deeper layers of the problem:

1. **First layer: event queue starvation.** The sequencer-engine path emitted 6+ events per block through the event system. Derivation events would pile up ahead of them, causing 200ms+ FCU-with-attrs delays.
   - **Fix:** Bypass the event system for the sequencer-engine path. Added direct-call methods (`StartBuild`, `SealBuild`, `ProcessPayload`) on `EngineController`, invoked synchronously by the sequencer.

2. **Second layer: seal phase still delayed.** Even with direct calls for build-start, the seal phase timer fired mid-drain during large span batches. The main `select` loop couldn't service `sequencerCh` until `Drain()` returned.
   - **Fix:** Replace `Drain()` with `DrainUntil()` + `RunAction()` direct cal...

## Summary

`L2Client.L2BlockRefBy{Label,Number,Hash}` and `SystemConfigByL2Hash` previously fetched the entire L2 block (`eth_getBlockByX` with `fullTx=true`) just to read a handful of header fields and decode the first transaction (the L1 info deposit). ## Description

op-conductor's RPC server inherited go-ethereum's default HTTP request body limit. This caps the size of payloads that op-node commits to the conductor via `conductor_commitUnsafePayload` (and any other HTTP RPC request).

This PR raises op-conductor's limit to 15MB.

## Changes

- **`op-service/rpc`**: Add a `WithHTTPBodyLimit(limit int)` option to the RPC `Handler`. When set (`> 0`), it calls `srv.SetHTTPBodyLimit(...)` on the underlying go-ethereum RPC server. When unset, the go-ethereum default is used, so **all other op-service RPC consumers are unaffected**.
- **`op-conductor`**: Pass `oprpc.WithHTTPBodyLimit(15*1024*1024)` in `initRPCServer`.

## Notes

- The limit is currently hardcoded at 15MiB. If a configurable CLI flag / env var is preferred, that can be added as a follow-up.
- Comments intentionally avoid naming the specific upstream default value so they don't go stale if go-ethereum changes it.

## Tests

- `go build ./op-service/rpc/... ./op-conductor/...` passes.
- `go test ./op-service/rpc/...` passes.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Summary

- Make the DA challenge contract optional for Keccak256 commitments, supporting permissioned Validium chains where ZK proofs (e.g., op-succinct/SP1) provide cryptographic data integrity without on-chain challenges
- Previously, `validateAltDAConfig` required `DAChallengeAddress != address(0)` for Keccak256, forcing users to pass a dummy address as a workaround
- Change `fetchChallengeLogs` guard from commitment-type-based to address-based, which is semantically correct ("skip if no challenge contract")

## Motivation

In a permissioned Validium model, the chain operator runs the DA server and ZK proofs verify `keccak256(data) == commitment` inside the zkVM. The on-chain challenge mechanism is redundant because there is no untrusted party to challenge and data integrity is already proven cryptographically.

## Test plan

- [x] `TestValidateAltDAConfig` — 6 table-driven cases covering all commitment type + address combinations
- [x] `TestFetchChallengeLogsNoChallengeContract` — verifies Keccak256 + zero address skips receipt scanning
- [x] Existing `TestConfig_Check`, `TestAdvanceChallengeOrigin`, and genesis config tests still pass

This change fixes two `Print results (failures)` commands in `.circleci/continue/main.yml` by switching:

  `cat "$LOG_DIR/failed/*.log" || true` to `cat "$LOG_DIR"/failed/*.log || true`.

  The previous form quoted the glob, so `*.log` was treated literally and did not expand. In failing runs, CI printed `No such file or directory` in this step even though `failed/*.log` artifacts were actually present. This made failures harder to diagnose.

  This is a minimal, low-risk fix: it only restores expected shell glob behavior for failure-log printing. It does not change test execution or job pass/fail semantics (`|| true` is unchanged).

## Summary

- Adds `kona-karst-derive`, a flat, inlined OP Stack derivation pipeline that replaces the staged `kona-derive` architecture
- Single `Pipeline` struct holds all intermediate state as fields; all derivation steps are direct method calls (no trait-based stage composition)
- Only supports **Holocene+**, removing all hardfork branching: always uses channel assembler, batch validator, Fjord size limits, and Holocene frame pruning

|---|---|

| `BatchStream.is_active()` guard | Always active |
| `FrameQueue` conditional Holocene pruning | Always prune |

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Eliminates multi-second sequencer stalls during batch derivation. Under load (e.g. consolidating a 4000+ block span batch), the sequencer's block production could be delayed by 3+ seconds, blowing past the block deadline. This fix makes reliable block production at 1s block times feasible even under heavy derivation load.

Closes #19263

## Journey & final root cause

The fix evolved through several iterations as we discovered deeper layers of the problem:

1. **First layer: event queue starvation.** The sequencer-engine path emitted 6+ events per block through the event system. Derivation events would pile up ahead of them, causing 200ms+ FCU-with-attrs delays.
   - **Fix:** Bypass the event system for the sequencer-engine path. Added direct-call methods (`StartBuild`, `SealBuild`, `ProcessPayload`) on `EngineController`, invoked synchronously by the sequencer.

2. **Second layer: seal phase still delayed.** Even with direct calls for build-start, the seal phase timer fired mid-drain during large span batches. The main `select` loop couldn't service `sequencerCh` until `Drain()` returned.
   - **Fix:** Replace `Drain()` with `DrainUntil()` + `RunAction()` direct cal...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## TL;DR

OP Stack headers use **op-geth receipts-trie rules** (pre-Canyon: **omit `deposit_nonce`** in the trie leaf even when it’s on the in-memory deposit receipt). Reth’s **background receipt-root task** builds the trie with **`encode_2718`**, which **includes** the nonce → wrong root. Consensus **trusted that precomputed `(receipts_root, bloom)` when `Some`**, so nodes **rejected valid blocks** (`receipt root mismatch`). This PR fixes trie hashing for OP and **stops using `receipt_root_bloom` for validation** (parameter kept for upstream API only).

---

## Problem

### Trie encoding mismatch (Regolith, pre-Canyon)

For deposit receipts after Regolith, **`deposit_nonce`** may be present on the execution receipt, but **before Canyon** the receipts trie must match **op-geth `Receipts.EncodeIndex`**: trie value matches the usual typed receipt shape and **does not include** `deposit_nonce` when **`deposit_receipt_version` is unset**. After Canyon, **`deposit_receipt_version`** is set and the nonce is included in the trie per spec.

Generic tooling (e.g. **`encode_2718` → ordered trie**) encodes the nonce → **different `receiptsRoot`** than the block header for other...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Replace bare `return err` calls in `recordsBook.prune()` with wrapped
errors using `fmt.Errorf("%w")` to include context about which operation failed.

## Motivation

Without context, errors from `prune()` are indistinguishable in logs —
a single `return err` gives no indication whether the failure was during
query, batch creation, record deserialization, or commit. This makes it
difficult to diagnose peer store issues in production nodes.

## Changes

- Query: `"failed to query records for pruning"`
- Batch init: `"failed to create batch for pruning"`
- UnmarshalBinary: `"failed to unmarshal record during pruning"`
- Batch commit (loop): `"failed to commit prune batch"`
- Batch re-init (loop): `"failed to create new batch during pruning"`
- Batch delete: `"failed to delete expired record <key>"`
- Final commit: `"failed to commit final prune batch"`

## Testing

`go build ./op-node/p2p/store/...` passes cleanly.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
This PR addresses a state inconsistency in `op-revm` where deposit transactions of type `Create` in the Bedrock hardfork (Pre-Regolith) failed to increment the sender's nonce if the execution resulted in a `Halt` (e.g., `OutOfGas`).

### The Bug
In `OpHandler::execution_result`, halted deposits were only converted to bubble-up errors if the `REGOLITH` hardfork was enabled. For `BEDROCK`, the handler returned `Ok(Halt)`, which bypassed the `catch_error` logic where the nonce bump occurs.

### The Fix
1. Modified `OpHandler::execution_result` to return an `Err(OpTransactionError::HaltedDeposit)` for all halted deposit transactions.
2. Renamed `OpTransactionError::HaltedDepositPostRegolith` to `OpTransactionError::HaltedDeposit` for correctness across forks.

Verified with regression test `test_halted_deposit_tx_bedrock_nonce_bump`.
@rakita

content_signals.body_excerpt:
**Description**

Tolerate transient `get_logs` failures at the L1 head in the kona node-service `L1WatcherActor`.

The `head_stream.next()` → `l1_provider.get_logs(filter.select(head.hash))` sequence in `actors/l1_watcher/actor.rs` is racy on chains with rapid head reorgs or load-balanced RPC endpoints with propagation lag: the head hash can disappear from the responding node between the two calls, and the upstream returns `-32001 block not found`. The `?` propagates it, the actor task returns `Err` and is dropped, which closes `block_signer_sender` and pulls down the network actor too — surfaced by `rollup_node` as `Critical error in sub-routine`.

Demote this single `get_logs` failure to `warn!` + `continue`. The SystemConfig log scan is idempotent across head ticks, so skipping one is safe and the next head update retries. All other error paths in the watcher are unchanged.

**Additional context**

Most visible against fast L1s and load-balanced RPC endpoints where the node serving `get_logs` lags the node that emitted the head. The error is transient by construction, so retrying on the next tick is the correct recovery.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Description

op-conductor's RPC server inherited go-ethereum's default HTTP request body limit. This caps the size of payloads that op-node commits to the conductor via `conductor_commitUnsafePayload` (and any other HTTP RPC request).

This PR raises op-conductor's limit to 15MB.

## Changes

- **`op-service/rpc`**: Add a `WithHTTPBodyLimit(limit int)` option to the RPC `Handler`. When set (`> 0`), it calls `srv.SetHTTPBodyLimit(...)` on the underlying go-ethereum RPC server. When unset, the go-ethereum default is used, so **all other op-service RPC consumers are unaffected**.
- **`op-conductor`**: Pass `oprpc.WithHTTPBodyLimit(15*1024*1024)` in `initRPCServer`.

## Notes

- The limit is currently hardcoded at 15MiB. If a configurable CLI flag / env var is preferred, that can be added as a follow-up.
- Comments intentionally avoid naming the specific upstream default value so they don't go stale if go-ethereum changes it.

## Tests

- `go build ./op-service/rpc/... ./op-conductor/...` passes.
- `go test ./op-service/rpc/...` passes.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Summary

- Adds `kona-karst-derive`, a flat, inlined OP Stack derivation pipeline that replaces the staged `kona-derive` architecture
- Single `Pipeline` struct holds all intermediate state as fields; all derivation steps are direct method calls (no trait-based stage composition)
- Only supports **Holocene+**, removing all hardfork branching: always uses channel assembler, batch validator, Fjord size limits, and Holocene frame pruning
- Re-exports traits and error types from `kona-derive` for compatibility

### Key simplifications vs kona-derive

| kona-derive | karst-derive |
|---|---|
| `ChannelProvider` mux (bank vs assembler) | Always assembler (inline) |
| `BatchProvider` mux (queue vs validator) | Always validator (inline) |
| `BatchStream.is_active()` guard | Always active |
| `FrameQueue` conditional Holocene pruning | Always prune |
| Fjord vs Bedrock `MAX_RLP_BYTES_PER_CHANNEL` | Always Fjord (`100_000_000`) |
| Recursive signal-based reset | Single flat `reset()` method |

- [x] `cargo clippy -p kona-karst-derive` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] Port key unit tests from kona-derive stages

## Summary
- Remove the `--interop.activation-timestamp` CLI flag from the supernode
- Instead, derive the interop activation timestamp from the rollup configs of the configured virtual nodes
- The rollup config already contains `interop_time` which is the source of truth

This eliminates a manual configuration step when deploying interop supernodes. Currently, operators must manually set `--interop.activation-timestamp` on the supernode even though the same information is already in the rollup config that the supernode loads. Without this flag, interop cross-chain verification silently doesn't start.

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify supernode starts interop activity without `--interop.activation-timestamp` flag when rollup config has `interop_time` set
- [ ] Verify supernode still works when `interop_time` is not set in rollup config (interop disabled)
- [ ] Run existing supernode tests

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Eliminates multi-second sequencer stalls during batch derivation. Under load (e.g. consolidating a 4000+ block span batch), the sequencer's block production could be delayed by 3+ seconds, blowing past the block deadline. This fix makes reliable block production at 1s block times feasible even under heavy derivation load.

Closes #19263

## Journey & final root cause

The fix evolved through several iterations as we discovered deeper layers of the problem:

1. **First layer: event queue starvation.** The sequencer-engine path emitted 6+ events per block through the event system. Derivation events would pile up ahead of them, causing 200ms+ FCU-with-attrs delays.
   - **Fix:** Bypass the event system for the sequencer-engine path. Added direct-call methods (`StartBuild`, `SealBuild`, `ProcessPayload`) on `EngineController`, invoked synchronously by the sequencer.

2. **Second layer: seal phase still delayed.** Even with direct calls for build-start, the seal phase timer fired mid-drain during large span batches. The main `select` loop couldn't service `sequencerCh` until `Drain()` returned.
   - **Fix:** Replace `Drain()` with `DrainUntil()` + `RunAction()` direct cal...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

`L2Client.L2BlockRefBy{Label,Number,Hash}` and `SystemConfigByL2Hash` previously fetched the entire L2 block (`eth_getBlockByX` with `fullTx=true`) just to read a handful of header fields and decode the first transaction (the L1 info deposit). For large blocks this can waste >99% of the bandwidth and CPU. These methods sit on hot paths: engine FCU validation, attributes builder, sync startup.

This PR introduces a minimal-fetch path that downloads only the header (`fullTx=false`) plus the first transaction in a single batched JSON-RPC round trip. By-hash is naturally consistent. By-label / by-number do an optimistic consistency check (`header.Transactions[0] == tx.Hash()`) and refetch only the tx by the resolved hash on mismatch (rare race under reorg / new head).

Follow up to #20531

## What changes

- **`eth.PayloadToInfo`** — new wrapper making `*ExecutionPayload` satisfy `BlockInfo` (now possible because the precursor PR removed `Header()` / `HeaderRLP()` from the interface).
- **`derive.BlockRefFromHeaderAndDeposit`** and **`derive.SystemConfigFromHeaderAndDeposit`** — new inner helpers operating on `(BlockInfo, *types.Transaction)`. The existing `Pa...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

op-deployer was creating a large number of files under --cache-dir because artifact bundles were extracted into fresh temporary directories on each run. Even when the same contracts/artifact locator were reused, only the downloaded archive was cached;
the extracted project directory was not. This also meant forge could not reliably reuse its compilation cache across repeated --use-forge commands.

This PR adds a persistent artifact resolution cache so repeated commands using the same artifact content reuse the same extracted project.

## What Changed

- Adds `artifacts.Resolve`, returning the artifact FS plus explicit project/cache metadata.
- Keeps `artifacts.Download` as a compatibility wrapper.
- Caches HTTP downloads under artifacts/archives.
- Caches extracted artifact projects by artifact content hash under artifacts/projects.
- Reuses embedded and HTTP artifact extractions across repeated commands.
- Leaves `file://` locators user-owned and uncached.
- Updates Forge paths to use explicit project roots instead of deriving paths from `fmt.Sprintf("%v", artifactsFS)`.
- Adds forge --root, --cache-path, and --out options so repeated Forge runs can ...
